theory.intro: Write a linear relationship as $y=mx+c$. The **coefficient of $x$** (the value $m$) is the **gradient** — it sets the line's direction and steepness — and the **constant term** (the value $c$) is the **$y$-intercept**, where the line crosses the $y$-axis. Reading $m$ and $c$ straight from two equations lets you compare their graphs without plotting.
facts: When $m$ is **positive** the graph is **increasing** (slopes up from left to right); when $m$ is **negative** it is **decreasing** (slopes down).
The **larger the magnitude** of $m$, the **steeper** the line — the sign sets the direction, the size sets the steepness.
Two lines with the **same coefficient of $x$** are **parallel**, whatever their constant terms.
Two lines with the **same constant term** cross the $y$-axis at the same place — the **same $y$-intercept**.

foundation.question_text: Is the graph of $y=5x-12$ increasing or decreasing?
Is the graph of $y=12-5x$ increasing or decreasing?
Where does the graph of $y=3x-4$ cross the $y$-axis?

foundation.solution_text:
$y=5x-12$ has coefficient $5$
$y=5x+48$ has coefficient $5$
Same coefficient, so parallel

$y=3x$ has coefficient $3$
$y=x$ has coefficient $1$
$3>1$, so $y=3x$ is steeper

development.question_text: Which two of $y=3x+1$, $y=-3x+1$ and $y=3x-5$ are parallel?
development.solution_text:
$y=3x+1$ has coefficient $3$
$y=-3x+1$ has coefficient $-3$
$y=3x-5$ has coefficient $3$
Same coefficient $3$, so $y=3x+1$ and $y=3x-5$ are parallel

development.question_text: Which two of $y=2x+2$, $y=4x+2$ and $y=7x-1$ have the same $y$-intercept?
development.solution_text:
$y=2x+2$ has constant term $2$
$y=4x+2$ has constant term $2$
$y=7x-1$ has constant term $-1$
Same constant term $2$, so $y=2x+2$ and $y=4x+2$ share the $y$-intercept

development.question_text: Which of $y=3x$, $y=x$ and $y=5x-2$ is the steepest?
development.solution_text:
$y=3x$ has coefficient $3$
$y=x$ has coefficient $1$
$y=5x-2$ has coefficient $5$
$5$ is the largest, so $y=5x-2$ is the steepest

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-3,xmax=3,ymin=-4,ymax=5,xtick={-3,-2,...,3},ytick={-4,-2,...,4},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-1.3:1.6,samples=2] {3*x} node[pos=0.9,right,text=blue!70!black] {$A$};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-2.8:2.8,samples=2] {x} node[pos=0.9,right,text=red!75!black] {$B$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph shows lines $A:y=3x$ and $B:y=x$. Which line is steeper?
development.solution_text:
$A:y=3x$ has coefficient $3$
$B:y=x$ has coefficient $1$
$3>1$, so line $A$ is steeper

$y=2x-1$ has coefficient $2$ (positive)
$y=3-4x$ has coefficient $-4$ (negative)
$y=x+6$ has coefficient $1$ (positive)
Only $y=3-4x$ is decreasing

mastery.solution_text:
$y=-4x+1$ has coefficient $-4$
$y=3x-2$ has coefficient $3$
compare magnitudes: $|-4|=4$, $|3|=3$
$4>3$, so $y=-4x+1$ is steeper

mastery.question_text: Which of $y=2x+5$, $y=-2x+1$ and $y=5x-2$ is parallel to $y=5-2x$?
mastery.solution_text:
$y=5-2x$ has coefficient $-2$
$y=2x+5$ has coefficient $2$
$y=-2x+1$ has coefficient $-2$
$y=5x-2$ has coefficient $5$
Same coefficient $-2$, so $y=-2x+1$ is parallel

mastery.question_text: Do $y=2x+5$ and $y=-3x+5$ have the same gradient, the same $y$-intercept, or both?
mastery.solution_text:
$y=2x+5$: coefficient $2$, constant term $5$
$y=-3x+5$: coefficient $-3$, constant term $5$
coefficients differ, so not the same gradient
constant terms match, so the same $y$-intercept
Same $y$-intercept only
